claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 19 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit! Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! I've started the assessment. To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your child.

**First question: How old is your child?**

This will help me determine which eligibility path applies to your situation:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children aged 20 or over typically don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, let me know about each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh hi, thanks for helping me with this! I have one child - Alex is 19 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So Alex is 19 years old and lives at home with you.

Since Alex is 19, they fall into the 16-19 age category. For children in this age group, Child Benefit eligibility depends on whether they're in approved education or training.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

Or has Alex recently left education or training (within the last few months)?

To help you answer:
- **Approved education** typically includes A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to level 3, and similar qualifications
- **Approved training** includes certain government-sponsored training programs
- The education/training must be full-time (more than 12 hours per week of supervised study or course-related work experience)

Please let me know which applies to Alex:
1. Currently in approved education or training
2. Recently left education or training
3. Neither - not in education/training and hasn't recently left

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is currently in education - he's studying for his A-levels at the local sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since Alex is studying for his A-levels at sixth form college, that's approved education. Now I need to check that the level and nature of the education meets the requirements for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! A-levels are definitely approved (non-advanced) education. Now I need to confirm a few more details about Alex's education:

**1. Is Alex's A-level course full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study or course-related work experience)

**2. Is this education funded or arranged by an employer,** or is there an agreement with an employer for a future job?

For most sixth form college students studying A-levels, the answer to question 1 is typically yes (it's full-time), and the answer to question 2 is typically no (not employer-funded). But I need to confirm this is the case for Alex.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's definitely full-time - he's there during the week like a normal school day. And no, it's not funded by any employer or anything like that - it's just regular sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! That's perfect. A-levels at a regular sixth form college, full-time, and not employer-funded - that all meets the approved education requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Does Alex work less than 24 hours per week?** (Or is he not in paid work at all?)

For students aged 16-19 in approved education, they can still qualify for Child Benefit even if they have a part-time job, as long as they're working fewer than 24 hours per week.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - he's just focused on his studies at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's absolutely fine. Now let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right?**

Specifically, I need to know if Alex is receiving:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Tax credits or similar benefits in his own name

Most students living at home and studying A-levels don't receive these benefits, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't get any benefits in his own name - nothing like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex doing an apprenticeship?**

You mentioned he's doing A-levels at sixth form college, which sounds like he's not in an apprenticeship program, but I need to confirm this as apprenticeships have different rules depending on the type and region.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not doing an apprenticeship - just the regular A-levels at sixth form.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now that I've confirmed Alex qualifies as an eligible child, I need to check a few things about you as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Do you normally live in the UK?**

To qualify for Child Benefit, you need to meet UK residency requirements. The simplest way is if you normally live in the UK. 

There are also other ways to meet the residency requirement (such as having settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or being a Crown servant abroad), but for most people, it's simply that they normally live in the UK.

Do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirement. Now I need to check about your responsibility for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Do you have responsibility for Alex?**

You mentioned earlier that Alex lives at home with you, which means you meet the responsibility requirement. Just to confirm: **Does Alex live with you?**

(Responsibility can also be shown by contributing financially to Alex's upkeep, but living together is the most straightforward way.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now let me check if there's anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- Anyone else

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. If someone else is claiming, you would need to agree on who should claim going forward.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Now I need to check where Alex is living to make sure that meets the requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex in any of these situations?**

1. **In hospital** (for an extended period)
2. **In care** (looked after by the local council)
3. **Being fostered** by you or someone else
4. **None of the above** - living normally at home with you

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like Alex is living normally at home with you, but I need to confirm this.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: None of those - he's just living normally at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue checking the requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question: Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

This would be things like the council paying for Alex's housing costs or care costs. For most families where the child lives at home normally, the answer is no - the council is not paying for the child.

Is that correct for your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - the council isn't paying anything for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Based on all the information you've provided, let me navigate to the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

Based on all the information you've provided, **you meet all the criteria for Child Benefit for Alex**.

### Why You're Eligible:

**Alex qualifies as an eligible child because:**
- At 19 years old, Alex falls within the 16-19 age range where eligibility can continue
- Alex is in full-time approved education (A-levels at sixth form college)
- Alex is not working (or working less than 24 hours per week)
- Alex is not receiving benefits in his own right
- Alex is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship

**You meet all the claimant requirements:**
- You normally live in the UK (Manchester)
- You have responsibility for Alex (living together)
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - If you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay some or all of the benefit back through tax. You can still claim and choose not to receive payments if you wish (to protect National Insurance credits).
5. **Payments** are typically made every 4 weeks

### More Information:
You can find the application form and more details here: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the application process?

reasonings: Alex is eligible as a qualifying child because: (1) Alex is 19 years old, which falls within the 16-19 age range for extended eligibility; (2) Alex is studying full-time A-levels at sixth form college, which is approved non-advanced education; (3) The course involves more than 12 hours per week of supervised study; (4) The education is not employer-funded; (5) Alex does not work (less than 24 hours per week); (6) Alex does not receive benefits in his own right (such as ESA or Universal Credit); (7) Alex is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant meets all requirements: (1) Normally lives in the UK (Manchester); (2) Has responsibility for Alex as they live together; (3) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (4) Alex lives normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered); (5) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (19 years old), who is in full-time approved education (A-levels at sixth form college). Alex meets all the qualifying child criteria for the 16-19 age group. The claimant meets all the eligibility requirements including UK residency, responsibility for the child, and no conflicting claims. The family is eligible for Child Benefit.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible as a qualifying child because: (1) Alex is 19 years old, which falls within the 16-19 age range for extended eligibility; (2) Alex is studying full-time A-levels at sixth form college, which is approved non-advanced education; (3) The course involves more than 12 hours per week of supervised study; (4) The education is not employer-funded; (5) Alex does not work (less than 24 hours per week); (6) Alex does not receive benefits in his own right (such as ESA or Universal Credit); (7) Alex is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant meets all requirements: (1) Normally lives in the UK (Manchester); (2) Has responsibility for Alex as they live together; (3) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (4) Alex lives normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered); (5) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (19 years old), who is in full-time approved education (A-levels at sixth form college). Alex meets all the qualifying child criteria for the 16-19 age group. The claimant meets all the eligibility requirements including UK residency, responsibility for the child, and no conflicting claims. The family is eligible for Child Benefit.
